items.q: Are all organisations in the private sector profit-driven?
items.correct: No — the private sector also includes non-profit social enterprises, charities and cooperatives that trade to pursue social aims rather than shareholder profit.
items.random_wrong: The private sector means businesses that keep their accounts secret.
items.why: IB Unit 1 distinguishes for-profit and non-profit private organisations; students collapse the two.

items.q: Can a business belong to more than one sector of the economy?
items.correct: Yes — vertically integrated firms can grow raw materials, process them and run shops, operating across primary, secondary and tertiary sectors at once.
items.misconception: Every business fits into exactly one sector.
items.random_wrong: Sectors are assigned to businesses by lottery each year.
items.why: Students force firms into single boxes; chains of production cross sector boundaries.

items.q: Do stakeholder conflicts mean a business is being run badly?
items.correct: No — conflicts, such as shareholders wanting dividends while employees want pay rises, are normal because interests differ; management's job is to balance them.
items.misconception: In a well-managed business all stakeholders want the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Stakeholder conflicts are settled by public referendum.
items.why: Recognising inherent trade-offs between stakeholder groups is a core IB evaluation skill.

items.q: Which sector includes software development and R&D consultancies?
items.why: It's Quaternary. “Primary” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is an INTERNAL stakeholder?
items.why: Correct answer: Employees. “Local community” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In an economy, 12% work in primary, 24% in secondary, 60% in tertiary. What % work in quaternary?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
